Branch: The undisclosed federal Canadian agency that tracks NYSE phenomena. No public name, no signage; everyone inside calls it 'the Branch.' The player's and Robert's employer.
OC Transpo: Ottawa's public transit operator. Runs the city's buses (and later light rail). On Bank Street, the diesel coaches grinding past are the unmistakable sound of an Ottawa weekday morning.
Globe: The Globe and Mail — Canada's national daily newspaper of record. Centre-right, broadsheet, the paper a mid-level federal bureaucrat reads with his coffee.
Métis: One of three constitutionally recognized Indigenous peoples of Canada, alongside First Nations and Inuit. Distinct nation arising from intermarriage between European fur traders and Indigenous (largely Cree, Ojibwe, and Saulteaux) communities, with its own language (Michif), culture, and homeland centred on the Prairies and extending east into Northern Ontario.
Shield country: The Canadian Shield. Vast Precambrian-rock landscape of boreal forest, lakes, and exposed bedrock covering most of Northern Ontario, Quebec, Manitoba, and the territories. Old land. Hard winters.
Sault: Sault Ste. Marie — small city on the St. Marys River at the Ontario–Michigan border. Steel town, paper town, gateway between the Great Lakes. Pronounced 'Soo.'
Thunder Bay: City on the north shore of Lake Superior in Northwestern Ontario. Roughly halfway between Toronto and Winnipeg by distance, but culturally closer to the Prairies than to the GTA. Home of Lakehead University.
Bank Street: Major north–south arterial running through central Ottawa — from Parliament Hill down through the Glebe and Centretown into the inner suburbs. Civil-service commute corridor, diners, second-hand shops, the city's working spine.
Diefenbunker: Cold War–era underground bunker in Carp, just outside Ottawa. Built 1959–1961 to house the federal government in the event of a nuclear strike. Decommissioned 1994; now a museum. Locally, a shorthand for the era when Ottawa built things to last forever.
Lakehead: Lakehead University, Thunder Bay. The regional university for Northwestern Ontario. Solid undergrad, no national reputation.
U of T: University of Toronto. Canada's most prestigious university. The jump from Lakehead to U of T's graduate programs is one most regional students don't make.
TTC: Toronto Transit Commission — the city's subway, streetcars, and buses, and the third-busiest transit system in North America. When the power dies it all stops at once, and the whole city walks home.
walkup: A low-rise apartment building with no elevator — you walk up the stairs to your unit. Older Toronto neighbourhoods are full of them: two to four storeys of postwar brick, a buzzer panel, a propped front door.
cell phone: In 2003, a cell phone meant a 'dumb' phone — calls and texts, no real internet, and pricey enough that plenty of people went without. Even with one, a back road like this would have no signal.
cordless phone: A landline handset with no cord tethering it to the base, so you could carry it around the house. The standard home phone of the era, before cells took over.
OPP: Ontario Provincial Police. The provincial force responsible for highways and rural and unincorporated Ontario, including Manitoulin — not the federal RCMP, and not a municipal service. It is why an off-duty Mountie here is a witness, not the responding officer.
